Her growth mindset has led to a change in her beliefs about smoking.
Cognitive dissonance led to her current belief.
The sunk-cost fallacy influenced her current belief.

Her highly authoritative parents likely contributed to her current belief.

Her growth mindset has led to a change in her beliefs about smoking.

Cognitive dissonance led to her current belief.

questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Melissa is a math teacher who teaches her students to solve certain types of problems by applying a formula in a systematic, step-by-step process. If a student skips steps in the process, they are more likely to solve the problem incorrectly.

Which of the following strategies best reflects Melissa’s approach?

Kori is a psychologist who works at a care center for older residents. She teaches classes on managing chronic pain using mindfulness and meditation. Kori also evaluates the eating habits, emotions, and relationships of residents to improve their quality of life and reduce their discomfort.

Which of the following fields of psychology is best reflected in Kori’s work practices?

Jenna used treats to train a pig to put a wooden coin into a toy machine. She began by giving a treat whenever the pig got close to the coin. Then, she only gave the pig a treat when it touched the coin with its snout. After that, she only gave the pig a treat when it picked up the coin with its teeth. Eventually, Jenna only gave the pig a treat when it picked up the coin and put it into the toy machine. 

Which of the following approaches did Jenna use?

questionList.questionTitle: Hindsight bias and tend and befriend - application
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
In his psychology class, Keenan was learning about the various ways people respond to and cope with stressful life events. While he believed that both men and women dealt with stress in the same way, his teacher explained that women are much more likely than men to reach out to friends to both give and receive support. Upon learning this, Keenan told a classmate this finding was totally predictable and obvious because women are typically more nurturing than men.

Keenan’s thinking is most clearly an example of which of the following concepts?

When Rowan was six years old, his father took him to see a tennis match. Rowan enjoyed learning the rules of the game and watching the players hit the ball back and forth across the net with their tennis racquets. 

Five months later, Rowan’s aunt took him to a park to play pickleball, a game similar to tennis. He had a great afternoon learning to hit the ball back and forth with the pickleball paddle instead of a tennis racquet. That night, Rowan told his parents that he had a great time playing “tennis” with his aunt. 

Which of the following concepts relates to Rowan’s schema in this scenario?

The social reciprocity norm

Dr. Xiang examined a 21-year-old patient who had fallen and hit their head. After a brain scan was conducted, Dr. Xiang discovered that the patient was missing their cerebellum. Dr. Xiang extensively interviewed the patient and their family and learned that the patient had a long history of balance and coordination problems. 

Based on this patient, Dr. Xiang tells her colleague that anyone who is missing their cerebellum will always experience balance and coordination problems.

Which of the following responses from Dr. Xiang’s colleague would be the most appropriate?

optionList.optionContent: Dr. Xiang cannot generalize her findings to the larger population because she used a single case study.
Dr. Xiang cannot make causal inferences because she conducted a naturalistic observation.

Dr. Xiang cannot generalize her findings to the larger population because she used a single case study.

Dr. Xiang can generalize her findings to the larger population because she had a representative sample.

D .

Dr. Xiang can make causal inferences because she conducted a longitudinal study.

Most infants learn the finger-to-palm grasp more quickly than the thumb-to-index-finger grasp because of early bottle feeding.
Grasping an object between the index finger and thumb requires a level of adrenaline in the prefrontal cortex not present at age six months.

A .

The age at which each grasping technique develops is dependent on the frequency of practice and modeling.

The exact age at which each grasping technique develops varies from infant to infant, but the order in which these physical stages occur is the same.

Most infants learn the finger-to-palm grasp more quickly than the thumb-to-index-finger grasp because of early bottle feeding.

D .

Grasping an object between the index finger and thumb requires a level of adrenaline in the prefrontal cortex not present at age six months.

questionList.questionTitle: Classical conditioning study set - research method
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Researchers at an advertising agency conducted a study to determine whether classical conditioning principles could influence consumers' preferences for a particular product. They randomly assigned 100 college students from a nearby university to one of two groups. 

Both groups watched a commercial for the same brand of shoes. One group watched a commercial that did not feature a celebrity endorsement, while the second group watched a commercial that featured a celebrity endorsement from a physically attractive celebrity. The participants were exposed to these ads multiple times a day for two weeks. 

At the end of the two weeks, the participants were asked to rate, on a scale of 1 to 10, how likely they were to purchase the brand of shoes advertised in the commercial they watched.

Which of the following statements most accurately describes why this study is an experiment?

optionList.optionContent: The researchers operationally defined the variables.
The participants were randomly assigned to one of two groups.
The sample was representative of the population being observed.
The researchers proposed a hypothesis.

questionOption:
A .

The researchers operationally defined the variables.

B .

The participants were randomly assigned to one of two groups.

C .

The sample was representative of the population being observed.

D .

The researchers proposed a hypothesis.

Glial cells provide support, insulation, and waste removal functions for neurons, while neurons create and transmit electrical impulses between cells.

Glial cells and neurons are both involved in processing sensory information, while primarily glial cells receive and process sensory input from the external environment.

D .

Glial cells are found in both the peripheral and central nervous systems, while neurons are only found in the central nervous system.

Students enrolled in an introductory psychology course want to examine the bystander effect on their campus. The students devise a study in which one of them will pretend to fall while riding a scooter. The students choose a confederate from among the group to ride the scooter. After each fall, the confederate will record whether they are helped.

The level of experience of the scooter rider
The location on the campus where the confederate fell
The students on campus who watched the confederate fall off the scooter

The number of people at the location when the confederate fell

B .

The level of experience of the scooter rider

C .

The location on the campus where the confederate fell

D .

The students on campus who watched the confederate fall off the scooter

While walking home, Leo saw his girlfriend hugging someone. His heart began to race, he began to sweat, and he felt tears in his eyes beginning to form. In response to those physical reactions, Leo thought, “I am feeling sad.” 

As he got closer to them, Leo recognized the person his girlfriend had been hugging as her brother. As his heart continued to pound, Leo felt happiness and he thought, “She still loves me.”

optionList.optionContent: Parasympathetic nervous system activity

questionOption:
B .

Parasympathetic nervous system activity

optionList.optionContent: Her fear of the test is the conditioned response (CR).
The tapping of the pen is the unconditioned stimulus (UCS).
Her fear when she hears the tapping when there is no test is the conditioned response (CR).

Her fear of the test is the conditioned response (CR).

B .

The tapping of the pen is the unconditioned stimulus (UCS).

C .

Her fear when she hears the tapping when there is no test is the conditioned response (CR).

D .

A test in this class is the conditioned stimulus (CS).

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Introduction

Researchers performed a study to determine if cell phone calls that interrupt in-person interactions between a mother and her child affect how well the child learns vocabulary words.

Participants

This study comprised 38 pairs of participants. Each pair comprised a mother and her two-year-old child. The average age of the mothers was 35.26 years (age range = 29.53 to 47.10 years; 19 mothers did not report their age). All the two-year-old children were developing along standard physical, social, and emotional timelines. The average age of the children was 27.05 months (2.25 years), and an equal number of boys and girls participated. All mother-child pairs lived in suburban communities surrounding the same major East Coast United States city; 90.9% of families self-identified as white while the remaining 9.10% self-identified as Hispanic, Pacific Islander, African American, or multiethnic.1 Each mother provided informed consent.

Method

At the beginning of the study, mothers and their children were placed in separate rooms. The children played in a supervised, child-friendly room. Meanwhile, in a separate room, the researchers trained the mothers regarding the meaning of two completely new words: blicking (meaning a type of bouncing) and frepping (meaning a type of shaking). The researchers made up these words and their meanings for the purpose of this study. To demonstrate blicking to their child, each mother was provided an 18-inch gender-neutral2 doll. To demonstrate frepping to their child, each mother could shake a 6-inch rattle. 

After providing each mother with a phone, which had been set to vibrate mode, the researchers trained each mother on how to answer the phone. The researchers told each mother to expect occasional phone calls during the study. During each call, the researchers provided one of three directions to the mother: 1) begin teaching the new word (teaching phase), 2) move to the next word (transition phase), or 3) engage in a conversation with the researcher as if talking with a friend (interruption phase). 

To initiate the teaching phase, the researchers called each mother and told her to teach the designated word to her child. Half of the mothers were told to teach blicking first while the other half were told to teach frepping first. The researchers interrupted half of the mothers while the mothers taught their first word and then interrupted the second half of the mothers while the mothers taught their second word. For the uninterrupted teaching phase, each mother received a transition call from a researcher that instructed her to start teaching the identified word. Researchers measured how long the children paid attention to the action in the video to determine whether the children learned blicking or frepping. The mothers closed their eyes during the video to avoid accidentally cueing the children to pay attention to the correct action in the video.

Results and Discussion

On average, the mothers repeated the word approximately 24 times in the interrupted teaching phase and approximately 20 times in the uninterrupted teaching phase. The difference was not statistically significant. 

The children paid attention to the word they learned in the uninterrupted session for an average of 0.63 seconds and in the interrupted session for an average of 0.50 seconds. The difference in means was statistically significant, suggesting that in-person interruptions between parent and child can affect how well young children learn vocabulary words.

Reed. J., Hirsh-Pasek, K., & Golinkoff, R.M. (2017). Learning on hold: Cell phones sidetrack parent-child interactions. Developmental Psychology, 53(8): 1438–1436.

Introduction

Psychologists were interested in how middle school and high school students experienced burnout in schools. In this study, the researchers examined the ways in which students’ reports of experiencing burnout were associated with school-related demands and resources.

Participants

The researchers collected data from 696 students in two secondary schools in the nation of Hungary. Of the students, 66% percent of the students were male and 34% were female.1 The participants’ average age was 16.2 years, and all students and parents agreed to participate in the study. Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data beyond nation of origin for participants.

Method

Participants responded to two quantitative measures collected electronically through a website. The first measure on student burnout asked questions that were grouped into three components of burnout: feelings of personal inadequacy at school (such as worrying about whether one will be successful in school), exhaustion at school (such as worrying about school during non-school hours), and cynicism toward school (such as loss of interest in school). 

The second measure examined students’ perceptions of the demands and resources of their schools. “Demands” referred to the mental challenges that students face (such as conflicts with teachers or other authorities), work style (such as the time of day a student completes schoolwork), and other emotional burdens related to school (such as worry and fear). “Resources” referred to social support in helping students develop feelings of autonomy or to discover opportunities to develop personally. 

All questionnaire items were answered on six-point Likert scales, with higher scores indicating more burnout.

Results and Discussion

In the first measure, the researchers found that female participants reported significantly higher levels of feelings of inadequacy and exhaustion than did male participants. No statistically significant difference in average scores for cynicism toward school was found. The means and standard deviations for each category are listed in Table 1.

Introduction

Researchers used a longitudinal design to examine the relationship between school burnout, feelings of connectedness1 or belonging at school, and feelings of victimization due to bullying among middle school and high school students.

Participants

The researchers gathered data from 363 students from eight middle schools or high schools in the nation of Italy. Most of the students were male (66%) with 34% being female.2 Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data beyond nation of origin for participants. The range of ages was 11 to 18 years old.

Method

Participants responded to several questionnaires at six-month intervals in late 2016 (Time 1), the middle of 2017 (Time 2), and again at the end of 2017 (Time 3). They completed three different quantitative measures during each of the three, six-month intervals: 

  • an 8-item survey that measured their sense of connectedness with their current school 

  • a 10-item survey that assessed their level of feeling victimized by different types of bullying (such as physical, verbal)

  •  a 9-item scale that measured school burnout 

Responses to all questionnaire items were made on 5-point Likert scales, with higher scores indicating more belongingness, victimization, and burnout, respectively.

Results and Discussion

The researchers found a positive correlation between school burnout and feeling victimized by bullying at each time point for both middle school and high school cohorts. The researchers found a negative relationship between connectedness and burnout over time for middle school students. Among high school students, only connectedness at Time 1 and Time 2 was negatively related to burnout at all three data collection points. Introduction

In this longitudinal study, researchers examined the relationship between sleep problems and students’ reported levels of burnout at school.

Participants

Data were collected from students enrolled in 29 different classes at 4 different middle schools in the nation of China. Data were collected in November 2018, May 2019, November 2019, and May 2020. The data represent 1,226 students who participated in all four data collection points, with an approximately equal number of boys and girls.1 Participants’ average age in November 2018 was 12.73 years. Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data beyond nation of origin for participants.

Method

Participants completed several paper-and-pencil surveys asking about their sleep problems and burnout. They answered 26 questions about their sleep during the six months prior to each data collection point. They also responded to 16 questions about burnout at school, which included questions about cynicism toward school (decreasing interest in school) and emotional exhaustion (feeling overwhelmed by school). Both surveys were completed at each data collection point, and all questionnaire items were answered on five-point Likert scales, with higher scores indicating a higher level of burnout.

Results and Discussion

In general, the researchers concluded that changes in burnout are related to changes in feelings of connectedness, which has implications for subsequent burnout. The association between burnout and feeling victimized by bullying significantly decreased over time.

Introduction

In this longitudinal study, researchers examined the relationship between sleep problems and students’ reported levels of burnout at school.

Participants

Data were collected from students enrolled in 29 different classes at 4 different middle schools in the nation of China. Data were collected in November 2018, May 2019, November 2019, and May 2020. The data represent 1,226 students who participated in all four data collection points, with an approximately equal number of boys and girls.1 Participants’ average age in November 2018 was 12.73 years. Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data beyond nation of origin for participants.

Method

Participants completed several paper-and-pencil surveys asking about their sleep problems and burnout. They answered 26 questions about their sleep during the six months prior to each data collection point. They also responded to 16 questions about burnout at school, which included questions about cynicism toward school (decreasing interest in school) and emotional exhaustion (feeling overwhelmed by school). Both surveys were completed at each data collection point, and all questionnaire items were answered on five-point Likert scales, with higher scores indicating a higher level of burnout.

Results and Discussion

The table depicts the significant correlation coefficients for sleep problems and burnout.

The data further showed an increase in students’ reports of sleeping problems at any one data collection point correlated with significantly higher levels of burnout reported at a subsequent time. Additionally, students’ reports of burnout at a given data collection point correlated with significant problems sleeping reported at a subsequent time. These patterns were consistent for both male and female participants. The results support the notion that sleep problems and burnout can become a concerning cycle among middle school students.

Liu, X., Zhang, L., Wu, G., Yang, R., & Liang, Y. (2021). The longitudinal relationship between sleep problems and school burnout in adolescents: A cross-lagged panel analysis. Journal of Adolescence, 88, 14-24.
